sentence: Subjective:
The patient, a 38-year-old male, presents with low back pain that started 2 weeks ago after lifting a box of books.
He describes the pain as achy and sharp in his low back, which radiates down his left leg.
He reports that the pain worsens with lumbar flexion.
He denies numbness, tingling, loss of sensation, or weakness in his legs.
He also denies any loss of control of his bladder or bowels.
He reports that his pain has been getting worse and that he has tried taking ibuprofen and Tylenol, which sometimes helps.
He denies any history of smoking and reports a sedentary job.
He is an Olympic weightlifter and has a history of playing soccer.
His father has back pain.
Past medical history:
The patient's father has back pain.
Review of systems:
The patient denies any symptoms in other organs that are relevant to his initial presentation.
Current medications:
None mentioned.
Objective:
Vital signs: Blood pressure is 130/70, respiratory rate is 18, and heart rate is regular and normal.
Physical exam:
The patient has midline tenderness at the L4-L5 disk space with right-sided lumbar paravertebral tenderness.
He has pain with lumbar flexion.
The supine straight leg test is positive.
The patient's patella and achilles reflexes are symmetrical.
Test Results:
An MRI shows a disk desiccation, a disk bulge with paracentral disk herniation resulting in moderate right neuroforaminal stenosis.
Assessment / Problem List:
The patient has lumbar back pain at the L4-L5 disk space due to a herniated disk with radiculopathy.
Plan:
I recommend a spinal injection with a strong NSAID and corticosteroid into the lumbar spine under fluoroscopy to help with the inflammation causing his pain.
I will also give him a prescription for the procedure and have him sign informed consent paperwork.
Follow-up:
I will schedule the procedure for today and review the informed consent paperwork with him before the procedure.
